channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

description.p: Rahul Garg argues that team-specific AI instructions should move from personal prompting habits into versioned repository artifacts. It is worth keeping because it turns AI-assisted coding consistency into an infrastructure problem with a concrete operating model for generation, review, and security workflows.

ul.li: Treat AI instructions like shared config: version them, review them in pull requests, and keep them in the repo.
Encode priorities and severity thresholds in the instructions so team judgment executes consistently across different developers.
Start with one high-leverage instruction, usually generation or review, then expand only after adoption exposes real gaps.
Use incidents, review drift, and repeated corrections as input for updating the instruction set, just like any other engineering infrastructure.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Dependency cooldown using the publish age as a signal for package resolution

description.p: PMG's dependency cooldown filters very recent package versions out of registry metadata during version resolution. It is worth keeping because it is a concrete, low-friction supply-chain defense that reduces exposure to freshly published malicious or broken releases without changing normal semver ranges.

ul.li: Cooldown acts at metadata resolution time, so the package manager falls back to older eligible versions automatically when the version range allows it.
If only too-new versions satisfy the range, the install fails explicitly instead of silently selecting a risky fresh publish.
The control currently depends on proxy mode and covers npm; direct tarball installs and some lockfile or cache-based flows bypass it.
A per-command skip flag preserves an emergency escape hatch without removing the default policy for normal installs.
